retrieved_docs.title: Fitness for Duty and Return to Work -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the European Union, the European Accessibility Act is equivalent to the ADA. Other regions have enacted requirements that address similar issues (see Table 1. Examples of Governmental Requirements Enacted in Various Nations).

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Two measures commonly used for epidemiological surveillance are morbidity and mortality. These measures describe the progression and severity of a given health event. They are useful tools to learn about risk factors of diseases and compare and contrast health events and between different populations. While similar and often related, morbidity and mortality, however, are not identical. Morbidity is the state of being symptomatic or unhealthy for a disease or condition. It is usually represented or estimated using prevalence or incidence. Prevalence describes the proportion of the population with a given symptom or quality. It is calculated by dividing the number of affected individuals by the total number of individuals within a specific population. It is usually presented as a ratio or as a percentage. On the other hand, incidence shows the frequency at which individuals within a specific population develop a given symptom or quality. It is calculated by dividing the number of NEW cases within a designated, particular period by the number of individuals within the population. When making this calculation, it is essential to remember to subtract the number of individuals already affected by the total number of individuals within a population.

retrieved_docs.title: Quality Of Life -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: QoL differs from the public health measure of health-related quality of life in that the latter is a measure that explores the connection between health and QoL. As there has been recognized difficulty with arriving at a universally accepted definition and measurement, recent studies have attempted to reframe QoL into separate domains. One example of this reframing is the "engaged theory," which takes QoL and divides it into 4 main domains: economics, culture, politics, and ecology. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Fitness for Duty and Return to Work -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fitness for work is a dynamic concept due to the changing nature of these 2 variables. Assessments are required primarily at the beginning of a job relationship, after a health problem emerges, or periodically when transferring positions within the same employment, especially for hazardous, physically demanding, or safety-sensitive jobs.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: From an etymological perspective, the word “epidemiology” can be divided into the Greek roots “epi,” “demos,” and “logos,” which respectively mean “upon,” “people,” and “the study of.” Historically, epidemiology has focused on population-level factors regarding communicable infectious diseases, but it has evolved to include non-communicable infectious diseases, chronic diseases, infant health, and environmental and behavioral health. Today, it is a wide-encapsulating umbrella that encompasses any health-related issues that may influence the overall health of a population, such as environmental exposures, injuries, natural disasters, and terrorism, to name a few. It is a multifaceted branch of medicine, fundamentally guided by systematic scientific inquiry via ratios, probabilities, and other statistical calculations, focusing on the incidence, distribution, and factors concerning diseases and health outcomes within a specific population.

retrieved_docs.title: Ghon Complex -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with latent tuberculosis are at risk of reactivation to active tuberculosis; this risk is highly dependent on the immune status of the host. [2] [4] In the developing world, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection plays a large role in the progression of tuberculosis via increasing immunosuppression as the infection continues without effective antiretroviral therapy. [6] Additionally, tuberculosis infection increases the levels of HIV replication. [6] Therefore, co-infection worsens both infections.

retrieved_docs.title: Family Dynamics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Interpersonal interactions among family members have lasting impacts and influence the development and well-being of an individual via psychosocial, behavioral, and physiological pathways. [2] Thus, family dynamics and the quality of family relationships can positively or negatively impact health.

retrieved_docs.title: Stages of Change Theory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Continuing the new behavior change is the focus of the maintenance stage. Here, individuals have maintained total abstinence from the adverse behavior for more than six months. As people progress through this stage, the more confident they become in their ability to sustain the positive lifestyle changes and the less tempted/fearful they feel of relapsing. They can maintain a new status quo and can remind themselves of the progress they have made. At times, they may have thoughts of returning to old habits; however, they resist the temptation and remain on track because of the positive strides they have made. People become skilled at anticipating potential triggers that may result in relapse and have constructed coping strategies to combat these situations in advance. Typically, people remain in this stage anywhere between six months to five years. [7] A longitudinal study from the 1990 Surgeon General’s Report showed that after 12 months of abstinence, 43% of people returned to their smoking habit. However, it was not until reaching the 5-year mark of abstinence that the risk for relapse dropped to 7%. [7] Individuals in this stage require support as they re-evaluate their reasons for change, acknowledge the success they’ve made thus far, consider the potential triggers for relapse, and subsequently create contingency plans to try and avoid relapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Lifestyle Medicine -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cost of healthcare in the United States has become prohibitive. At the time of this publication, it is estimated that the cost to provide care to the average adult is over $13,000 annually in the US. [3] The Centers for Disease Control (CDC) reports that six in ten Americans have a chronic disease, and two in four have two or more chronic diseases. These conditions account for approximately 75% of all healthcare costs. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Morbidity and mortality are two types of retrospective information that allows for continuous evaluation of the efficacy of either a specific health care system or an implemented intervention in place. For example, the use of maternal morbidity and mortality to gauge the risks of pregnancy and childbirth, as well as the efficacy of the health care they receive, are of vital importance. [2] In a related vein, accurate assessment of these measures are crucial to understanding and evaluating their impact and trajectory. Ultimately, mortality alongside morbidity allows epidemiologists to study further the burdens that a health event may place on a population. These metrics also allow stakeholders to more effectively prioritize which health events to tackle and allocate resources toward and proactively manage the potential onset of a health event.

retrieved_docs.title: Change Management In Health Care -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lippitt, expanding on Lewin’s original theory, developed the Phases of Change Theory, which includes the following change phases: Increasing awareness of the need for change Developing a relationship between the system and the change agent Defining the change problem Setting goals and action plans for achieving change Implementing the change Gaining staff acceptance and stabilizing the change Redefining the relationship of the change agent and the system [3] Kotter’s 8-Step Change Model, developed in 1995, includes the following steps for effective change management: Create a sense of urgency for change Form a guiding change team Create a vision and plan for change Communicate the changed vision and plan with stakeholders Enable action by removing barriers to change Generate short-term wins Build on the change Anchor the change in the organizational culture [3] Finally, Rogers’ Diffusion of Innovation Theory outlines the following 5 phases of change: [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Stages of Change Theory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: TTM has become one of the most commonly applied theoretical and clinical frameworks in mental health and is effective across a broad spectrum of problems, including smoking, alcohol abuse, addiction, weight control and exercise acquisition, sunscreen use, condom use, school bullying, to preventative measures such as medical screens like mammography and cancer screening. [1] This article explains the important concepts of TTM and how to use it. [1] [2] [3] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Stages of Change Theory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The processes of change explain how change occurs. The ten processes illustrate both the external and internal requirements needed to transition through the stages. Decisional balance considers the pros and cons of change. The further one progresses through the stages, the greater the pros of change are valued over the cons. Self-efficiency explains the level of confidence one has in executing and maintaining a positive change despite the temptation for relapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Statistics -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Eventually, accurate and reliable interpretation follows from properly conducted data analysis. This step focuses on generating correct information based on the findings while relating it to the context of the topic under study. The current generation of discoveries, conclusions, and hypotheses will make future researchers capable of studying its underlying issues and restarting the statistical process, creating a continuous cycle of collecting, organizing, analyzing, and interpreting data.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevalence -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In medical epidemiology, prevalence is defined as the proportion of the population with a condition at a specific point in time (point prevalence) or during a period of time (period prevalence). [1] Prevalence increases when new disease cases are identified (incidence), and prevalence decreases when a patient is either cured or dies.  Many times, the period prevalence will provide a more accurate picture of the overall prevalence since period prevalence includes all individuals with the condition between two dates: old and new (incident) cases, as well as those who were cured or died during the period. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Collaboration -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Collaboration has become an essential competency in the national and global plan for quality improvement.  There is a need to transform a culture of avoidance and isolation that continues to cause medical errors and lower patient outcomes.   A necessary strategy is to improve interrelationships and interactions between health care providers (HCP) and their teams in the clinical setting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

Collectively, these hierarchical and classificatory relationships demonstrate how gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth parameters intersect with broader perinatal disease categories, supporting clinical coding, epidemiologic surveillance, and comprehensive patient care for newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is categorized as a specific type of maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it is considered a subcategory under the broader classification of other conditions or status of the mother complicating these reproductive processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 263
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concepts described are tightly linked through hierarchical and associative relationships that clarify how gestational timing, perinatal disorders, and reproductive health services intersect. The specific gestational interval “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is connected to the outcome category “liveborn,” indicating that a liveborn infant can result from pregnancies reaching this stage. Conversely, the broader classification “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” lists “liveborn” as its parent, positioning liveborn as a more general category that encompasses such disorders. “Duration of gestation” is defined as an inverse‑isa of “liveborn,” meaning that the liveborn status is a particular instance within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. Encounters with health services for reproductive reasons (ICD‑10 Z30‑Z39) also cite “liveborn” as a parent, reflecting that liveborn infants are a key focus of reproductive health care encounters. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: The term “komplikace” (complication) is described as being able to be qualified by specific conditions, and the descriptors “Allowed qualifier” and “can be qualified by.” indicate that certain entities are permissible qualifiers for this category. In particular, the complication category can be qualified by diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), by other complications of pregnancy, and by death (التموت). This framing underscores that diabetic kidney disease, pregnancy‑related complications, and mortality are recognized as valid examples of medical complications.
